data.title: The consensus reached at the Hong Kong Consensus Conference: Is the cryptocurrency industry doomed?
data.description: <div class="share___SVvtl"><div>This episode of Wu Shuo's AMA focused on observations and feelings about the Hong Kong Consensus Conference, discussing the current state and future of the cryptocurrency industry. Guests included Colin Wu, editor-in-chief of Wu Shuo; Teddy, founder of XHunt and a DeFi expert; Leon Liu, CEO & CTO of Hubble AI; Esther, head of the Hong Kong ecosystem at Conflux; Haotian, a Web3 researcher; Albert Luxon, PM at a macro hedge fund; Cindy from JiaMi Technology; and well-known cryptocurrency KOLs such as Nana and Kirara. Guests shared their views on the future of the cryptocurrency industry and their feelings about the conference. With the market downturn and the rise of AI technology, the cryptocurrency industry is experiencing emotional fluctuations, and many are beginning to feel pessimistic about the industry's prospects. The guests generally believed that the "consensus" in the cryptocurrency industry has changed, and many traditional cryptocurrency projects are facing new challenges. Despite this, some believe that the notion of "the crypto world is finished" is overly pessimistic, and that the industry still has potential, especially in areas such as payments, compliance, and stablecoins. The combination of AI and Web3 is considered a crucial future development direction, particularly the fusion of decentralization and the AI ​​economy, which may bring new opportunities. Furthermore, the speakers shared many interesting and absurd anecdotes about the conference. Overall, although the market currently faces challenges, many participants remain hopeful for the future, looking forward to finding new development directions and potential growth areas. The speakers' statements do not represent Wu Shuo's views and do not constitute any investment advice. Please strictly abide by local laws and regulations. The audio transcription was done by GPT and may contain errors. Please listen to the full podcast on platforms such as Xiaoyuzhou and YouTube. Part 1: Sharing My Impressions of the Hong Kong Consensus Conference Colin: The Hong Kong government attaches great importance to the Consensus Conference, but it lacks industry hot topics and the atmosphere at the venue is rather cold. Colin: I didn't actually participate much in the event itself, but I can share a few observations. First, I also tweeted that day, and a friend joked that this was the first time I'd encountered an event where I had to buy my own alcohol—going to a bar and having to pay for it myself. I saw quite a few people saying the same thing in the comments, which I found quite amusing. To some extent, this also reflects that the Consensus Conference as a whole was a bit lackluster. The second point is an interesting observation. I think the Hong Kong government attaches great importance to this consensus conference. Perhaps they believe Hong Kong's international role has been relatively diminished, so they particularly value large-scale conferences initiated by overseas project teams. You see, many big names spoke at the conference, and several new policies were even announced. Judging from the overall atmosphere, the Hong Kong government does indeed attach great importance to this consensus conference. The third impression is that many foreigners did come. I saw Joseph Lubin and other representatives from overseas project teams in Hong Kong. However, overall, it feels like there weren't many truly grounded, practical issues. While the Hong Kong government attaches great importance to it, there are relatively few issues that reach the forefront of the industry. On the one hand, there aren't many hot topics in the industry as a whole at present. Two major current hot topics are prediction markets and stock tokenization. However, both face significant regulatory hurdles in Hong Kong. Therefore, despite their global popularity, these topics saw little practical implementation or in-depth discussion at this Consensus Conference in Hong Kong. Another point is the recent release of policies regarding RWA (Real-World Asset On-Chain) in mainland China. I noticed this topic was discussed extensively, including by figures like Xiao Feng. However, given that this Consensus Conference primarily featured overseas organizers and participants, the discussion on new developments in RWA in mainland China and Hong Kong was relatively low. Perhaps in a few months, at events hosted by companies like Wanxiang, there will be more discussion on these developments. Haotian: From "Everyone Talking Their Own Talk" to "Collective Pessimism": The Shift in Industry Sentiment at This Year's Consensus Conference. Haotian: At the last Consensus Conference, my most direct feeling was that everyone was talking about their own thing. For example, those involved in DeFi were shouting about RWA, or some were talking about AI, some were talking about community, and various sectors were all talking about it. But you would find that everyone was indeed doing their own thing, without forming a real consensus or a collective force. There was no major trend that everyone generally recognized and was optimistic about; that was my feeling last time. But this time, what I felt was that there was a "consensus"—that is, "the crypto world is finished" and "this industry is doomed." A unified sense of pessimism and cynicism pervaded the conference, which was a strong feeling I had. Of course, this doesn't represent my personal opinion. The reason I specifically attended this consensus conference so close to the Lunar New Year was because I originally thought it would be different from the more Asia-focused conferences in April and May. Those conferences were dominated by Chinese projects; I thought this one would have more overseas projects, who might be more optimistic overall or less easily swayed by emotions regarding the future. But in reality, I found it wasn't quite what I expected. The Chinese projects and KOLs I met did indeed have more pessimistic voices. However, I also socialized with some overseas project representatives at the conference, and they seemed to be in an even worse state. They gave me the impression that: emotions are irrelevant to you, the future of the industry is irrelevant to you, we're just doing our own thing, and you wouldn't understand anyway. Overall, there's a sense of apathy towards the industry, even a kind of complacency. I actually think this state is worse. It would be better if everyone felt the industry was doomed, collectively cursed it, and then went looking for new opportunities, than this superficial statement that the industry is doing well but actually showing no awareness of external emotions or a sense of urgency. Overall, I'm personally quite disappointed. Cindy: The Consensus Conference was like a "top club," a chance encounter with Bullish CEO, and the magical scene of Justin Sun's Banana NFT. Cindy: My experience attending the Consensus Conference was quite absurd, a double experience of absurdity and surprise. My first impression was that I really met a lot of top global figures at this conference; there were no retail investors, mostly core industry leaders. And you never know who's sitting next to you. For example, on the morning of the 11th, when I was listening to a Grayscale executive speak at the Consensus main venue, the man next to me was applying hand cream, and it smelled amazing. I added him on WhatsApp and asked him to send me the brand. Half an hour later, when he went on stage, I realized he was Tom Farley, the CEO of Bullish, the one who invested in CoinDesk. Also, while having coffee, I ran into Graham, the CEO of Securitize. The day before, BlackRock announced its purchase of UNI (Uniswap token), and Securitize assisted in completing the compliance review. The most outrageous thing was at a party on the evening of the 10th, where Justin Sun was also there. After dinner, he said he would give everyone a dessert and handed out a huge box. When we opened it, there was really only one banana inside. I thought it was incredibly absurd, yet also hilarious. Cat Brother: Haha, isn't that one of those NFTs he bought before? The one with the banana stuck on with tape. Cindy: Yes, that's right. Teddy: The main venue was quiet, while institutions were present; the diversion of funds and attention to AI has become a new "consensus." Teddy: Actually, this trip was very different from my previous Consensus Conferences. In the past, I would spend at least half a day or even a whole day at the main venue, but this time I spent very little time there, only about an hour. Because after looking around, I realized that many of the projects weren't familiar to everyone; they were more focused on institutional business and compliance-related projects. Talking to them, on the one hand, I didn't know much about the projects, and on the other hand, we didn't have many common topics. So, the only in-depth conversation I had at the main venue was a brief exchange with someone from Alibaba Cloud. On the contrary, I felt there was more interaction at some of the peripheral activities. For example, at BNBChain's event, I was able to meet quite a few people and have good communication opportunities. My most intuitive feeling about this Consensus Conference is: the era of institutions has arrived. I feel that institutions are entering, and retail investors are leaving. Once institutions and large funds enter, they will emphasize compliance, licenses, and regulatory frameworks, which are not actually friendly to retail investors. So, from my personal perspective, this Consensus Conference is like a relatively obvious turning point between institutions and retail investors. As for "is the crypto market finished?", my thought is that the crypto market will definitely not be finished. The spirit of decentralization has always been there, and the demand for decentralization has always existed, and this demand will exist for a long time and will be very strong. The biggest problem now is that AI has stolen a lot of attention and funding from the crypto world, and many builders have also turned to the AI ​​track. Therefore, the "consensus" at this Consensus Conference doesn't necessarily mean the crypto world is finished, but rather that we need to "shift to AI." I think there are two levels to this "shift to AI." The first is a complete transformation, no longer doing anything related to crypto, and directly entering the pure AI track, such as developing products around ecosystems like OpenAI. The second is the combination of Web3 and AI, such as creating AI agents, or using AI technology to serve the needs of the Web3 industry, such as using AI to help Web3 projects with deployment, growth, and operations. Overall, my feeling can be summed up in three keywords: institutionalization, shift to AI, and a generally low sentiment. Neither project teams nor KOLs seemed particularly enthusiastic. That's all for now. Nana: Chinese sessions focused on coin prices and bottom-fishing, while English sessions were more relaxed, with AI becoming a "standard narrative" for projects. Nana: I attended some Chinese and English sessions this time. Let's start with the Chinese sessions, mainly exchange-related events. I clearly sensed a certain sentiment: in the Chinese sessions, the core focus was still on coin prices. I was asked similar questions in almost every Chinese session, such as whether it was a good time to buy at the current price, or if there were any other worthwhile coins to invest in. Overall, people were more sensitive to market fluctuations, and their emotions were more influenced by price. Then, the English-language gathering I attended was organized by a friend, a meeting for KOLs and project teams. Many of the participants were from Dubai and Europe. In that setting, almost no one mentioned coin prices. It was mostly relaxed conversation, casual chatter about life and ideas. They barely discussed market views or price trends. So, I felt they were generally quite optimistic, or rather, not so easily swayed by short-term market sentiment. However, in the Chinese-language gathering, perhaps because everyone generally had larger positions, they were more sensitive to prices, and their emotions fluctuated more significantly. Looking at the overall trend, I actually quite agree with what Teddy just said. Previously, I thought that if the market was bleak, the number of projects would be much smaller. But this time, I attended many public chain events and found that there are still many projects underway, just of relatively concentrated types. They can be roughly categorized into a few types: First, AI. Basically, no matter what sector they're in, they'll add some AI elements. Whether it's payments or RWA, they'll overlay the AI ​​narrative and lean in that direction. Additionally, I've seen some founders who were originally working on Web3 switch directly to pure AI projects. This is probably because more funds are flowing into AI now. But I think that for those running projects, it's still important to understand what they are truly good at. Because I feel that AI and Web3 are actually different in terms of financing logic and gameplay. Blindly transforming might not be a good thing. Esther: Prediction Markets and Stablecoins Clustering Together, Market Makers Returning, the Battle for the Chinese-Speaking Market Under the International Consensus Conference. Esther: The following views represent only my personal opinion and do not represent any institution. This time attending the Consensus Conference gave me a very different feeling. First, I found that there are a lot of people doing prediction markets; it feels like there are hundreds of teams doing it. The participants come from diverse backgrounds, including projects incubated by compliant securities firms, and even teams with experience in offline gambling, such as those from Macau casinos, who are also working on prediction markets. They recognize that prediction markets could become a "new type of casino" on the blockchain, and they already have organic traffic; besides traditional online gambling, they don't want to miss out on this potential explosive growth area on the blockchain. The second observation is the large number of Chinese teams working on stablecoins. It's not just about common USD stablecoins, but also non-USD stablecoins. I even heard of a case where a team developing a live-streaming platform, primarily serving the European, American, and Middle Eastern markets with tipping features, wanted to issue their own stablecoin. They didn't want to rely entirely on other stablecoins for settlement and wanted to control the clearing and issuance rights themselves. You'll find that even application-layer teams now want to become stablecoin issuers. The third observation is that market makers have become noticeably more active and have started "operating" again. Because there are fewer projects and lower liquidity, their performance pressure is likely greater, so they are more proactive in contacting project teams. Another impression is that this Consensus Conference was indeed very international. People from various regions and sectors attended, including Europe, America, Japan, South Korea, and Southeast Asia. Another important point is that large European and American exchanges like Coinbase and Kraken are also paying significant attention to the Chinese-speaking market. They have also recognized its potential. Although the Chinese-speaking market is already highly competitive, and it may not be easy to surpass local platforms in terms of product and user experience, given recent fund and capital movements in the Chinese-speaking region, they also want a piece of the pie. Because in the end you'll find that Chinese users have a high trading frequency and high capital activity. Moreover, these overseas exchanges also have advantages that some offshore platforms don't have, such as stock tokenization and other business directions. Albert: Consensus Conference from a Secondary Market Perspective — Trading Team Shrinkage, Traditional Institutional Takeover, and Concerns about Liquidity Crisis Albert: Since most people are talking from a primary market perspective, I'll talk from a secondary market, fund trading perspective. I personally work in a fund in Singapore, so I'll briefly discuss this from the perspective of funds and trading teams. First, attending this Consensus Conference, I noticed a very interesting phenomenon: from the perspective of trading and funds, this year's overall quality was significantly worse than the previous one. The previous conference was at least twice the size of this one. When I arrived at the main venue, my first thought was: why is there only one floor left? I even checked the exhibitor list, and there were almost no new faces. The attendees were mostly familiar faces—media, institutions, and exhibitors from traditional market backgrounds—almost everyone I'd seen before. You get the feeling—"It's you again, always the same old you." Whether it's infrastructure, payments, revenue-generating products, or compliance channels, there's been little change; there's almost no fresh blood. This is quite awkward. However, on the side events side, I noticed another phenomenon. There were several large-scale institution-only summits surrounding this Consensus Conference. I attended several, especially Monday's. I found that the number of trading teams participating was about one-third less than before. Why? Because after the market rally last October, many teams using arbitrage, funding rate, and carry strategies were wiped out. Using the word "wiped out" is not an exaggeration. Even in this environment, over 85% of the teams I encountered were still using the "old three" strategies, without any significant upgrades. Strategies with truly high barriers to entry, such as market making, long-short, and volatility, have become extremely rare, and even rarer as time goes on. In volatility trading, I saw almost no newcomers, only familiar faces. In market making, I still saw a few newcomers, such as a few teams from France working on on-chain liquidity deployment at DWS events. But in long-short and volatility, there were virtually no new entrants. This gives me the impression that the market has lost its appeal to new trading teams, or rather, it has matured to a point where newcomers have no interest in entering. Regarding institutional business, traditional lending and custody services didn't receive much attention at this Consensus conference. The two areas with the most attention were payments and the transfer of RWA asset exposure. The problem is that both of these directions are essentially becoming "license businesses." Payment requires licenses, a robust AML/CFT system, and strong credit; RWA is even more so, with the core being the issuer and compliance qualifications. Some Chinese institutions I met at the event said this is "Old Deng's business"—only those with deep traditional financial backgrounds and established credit can do it. What does this mean? It means that many institutions' businesses are being gradually taken over by institutions with traditional financial backgrounds. This trend is completely different from a few years ago. For institutions like Robinhood, which already have a traditional market foundation and are now entering the crypto market, reverse penetration is relatively easy. I also met people with backgrounds from traditional markets like the NYSE and HKEX at the event. They were very confident about entering the RWA or asset servicing field. For them, it's simply a transfer of skills. From a strategy adoption perspective, the current problem is that, apart from the "old three" strategies, the capacity in other areas is very limited. The figures I heard at the event showed that many strategies had a capacity of less than $10 million, mostly in the $10-30 million range. I hardly saw any teams capable of handling large asset management scales. This is completely different from the past. Another significant change is that LPs and FoFs now place great emphasis on "cross-market capabilities." Whether in a traditional or crypto market context, they all want their teams to be able to trade not only crypto but also cross-asset classes like US stocks and commodities. This is a very clear trend. Let me also complain about the side events at the institutional events. The number of attendees at these institutional events was significantly lower than at the KOL events, much quieter. Many so-called "institutional events" actually had attendees from wallet development, infrastructure, and even affiliate marketing teams, but the actual trading teams probably made up less than 20%. This is very rare. Even more dramatically, last Thursday, after returning to my hotel from an institutional event, my phone suddenly vibrated—a very large institution had suspended repayments. The market was already in a bearish phase at the time, and this news immediately jolted me awake. When I looked at the name of the institution, it was one I knew very well; a friend of mine held a high-ranking position there, and it was a large and reputable organization. My first thought was that if it really collapsed, the impact might be even greater than 3AC. After the meeting, as soon as this news broke, everyone was speculating about who would be next. In summary, my core observation is that the entire market is undergoing a transition period led by traditional financial teams. This transition presents an opportunity for those capable of adapting, but for teams unable to handle this change, the growing pains will be very prolonged, and they may even be wiped out entirely. Kirara: The Consensus Conference has shifted from "finding projects" to "finding food," with KOLs collectively turning to AI for self-help. Kirara: I've actually attended many conferences—Web3 events in Singapore, Dubai, Japan, Taiwan, and Hong Kong last April. I think the biggest difference between last year and this year is: before, as a KOL, I would meet with agencies and project teams to see what new moves and promotions were happening. Everyone was looking for "where to find projects." But this time, people aren't looking for projects; they're looking for food. They're really hungry. With Bitcoin's price plummeting like this, hardly anyone talks about Bitcoin anymore, or even about cryptocurrency trading. Before, people talked about "what projects you've been researching lately," "what airdrops you've been grabbing," and "where you've been making money." Now they talk about "what you've been busy with lately" and "what you've been playing around with lately." The answer I got was—many people are researching Vibe Coding. People used to say don't be product managers, but now everyone's a product manager. The bear market has given everyone a lull, a time to calmly study AI, like using AI programming tools to create small websites or products. I even saw a KOL use AI to create a program that allows you to control the direction of rain with gestures, a reference to a movie scene. People are no longer focused on which coin will increase 100x, nor do they care whether Bitcoin can reach $100,000 or set a new high. They're even too lazy to criticize the US or Trump anymore. This is the state I've observed among KOLs. Judging from the events, the most crowded and extravagant ones are still those organized by exchanges. I went to two, Binance and BitMart. Binance was packed; BitMart booked out the entire Lan Kwai Fong venue, which was truly extravagant. It seems the exchanges are still the wealthiest. There aren't many side events organized by project teams. One was quite unique, where several projects held events together, setting up round tables and having lion dance performances in one hall, making it look like a wedding. The organizer itself is a project team specializing in event organization, which impressed me quite a bit. Part Two: Is the Crypto World Really "Doomed," and What Opportunities Remain? Kirara: The Ebb of Old Narratives and "AI Infrastructureization"—The Crypto World Isn't Over, But the Game Has Changed. Kirara: First, I need to understand the statement "the crypto world is dead" in two parts. I don't agree that the entire industry is dead. But if some old things are indeed dead, then I think that's true. For example, the narrative surrounding Bitcoin has actually changed. Initially, Bitcoin emphasized privacy, decentralization, and censorship resistance, which supported its early growth. Later, its surge was driven by the "digital gold" narrative. However, the momentum driving its current surge seems to be weakening. In other words, the force supporting the surge through the old narrative is, to some extent, "finished." Regarding projects, I've noticed some projects that have been building for a long time but haven't achieved a TGE (Tencent Execution Event) or any particularly outstanding results, yet they continue building. But the pace of the crypto world is incredibly fast; sometimes I feel it's like a talent show for internet celebrities. The window of opportunity for a concept might only be three to four months. If you don't break out of the niche and "debut" within those three or four months, it's very difficult to regain popularity based on the original concept later. For example, some sectors that were very popular recently, such as certain lightweight product narratives, were almost completely ignored at this Consensus Conference. You'll find that if you miss the boat and don't debut during that talent show period, it becomes extremely difficult to compete later using your previous advantages. The same applies to AI. Last year, many so-called AI projects used AI as a core selling point. For example, you could use AI to give instructions to buy and sell cryptocurrencies, or create AI virtual boyfriends/girlfriends, or companion-type products—these were typical AI narratives last year. But this year, I think things have changed. Previously, AI was a "feature of the project," now it has become the "foundation of the project." You no longer distinguish between AI projects and non-AI projects; in fact, it's strange if a project doesn't have AI. AI is transforming from a label into an infrastructure. So for project teams, the pressure is immense. They must constantly keep up with the pace and the latest trends. If we look to new tracks, I think the crypto market has hope; but if we remain stuck in old tracks and old narratives, then from that perspective, the crypto market is indeed "finished." Albert: The crypto market isn't finished, it's maturing—the trend of financial infrastructureization and "US stock market-ization" is reshaping the industry. Albert: I don't really agree with the term "finished." Rather than saying it's finished, I prefer to use one word to describe it—maturity. What does this mean? From a macro perspective, the crypto market has actually been shrinking since 2024. Many directions have been validated, and the market has gradually reached a consensus on which areas can generate stable cash flow and which have a higher probability of success. During my recent trip to Hong Kong, I also spoke with some friends, who summarized four areas for me: payments, transactions, compliance, and risk control. If you look at the booths at this year's Consensus Conference, you'll find that they are basically all focused on these four areas. Compared to 2024, when these areas were mainstream but not absolutely dominant, this time almost all established companies and projects are working around these four areas. What does this mean? It means the market has clearly defined "what crypto can do." It's being seen as a new generation of financial infrastructure, not a boundless innovation testing ground. The space for innovation is shrinking, and it's increasingly revolving around finance itself. This is also why payments and RWA were so prominent at this conference. Because other new narratives have largely been explored. What follows is more of a "license business"—whoever has compliance capabilities and credit backing will have an advantage. However, I believe some areas do face the risk of "shrinking" or even "dying out," such as crypto native listing, which specifically involves listing and issuing tokens on crypto exchanges. I spoke with some relatively stable project teams recently, and their thinking is very clear: if a project grows large, their first priority won't be listing on a token, but rather going public, such as on Nasdaq or the Hong Kong Stock Exchange. This is why many people from traditional exchanges have been particularly active this time. Nasdaq, the NYSE, and the ICE system are all promoting asset tokenization. If traditional finance can provide stronger credit backing, then excellent projects will naturally be more willing to go through the sponsorship of the eight major investment banks and list on the US stock market. Listing on the US stock market provides significantly better valuation, liquidity, and long-term funding support than simply listing on a cryptocurrency exchange. This will bring about a crucial shift in perception: projects listed on the US stock market are considered "good projects," while those not listed on traditional capital markets may be labeled as risky. I even spoke with Bloomberg, and South Korean retail investors are now more inclined to buy crypto-related stocks listed on the US stock market than projects simply listed on exchanges. Traffic on South Korea's four major local exchanges has declined significantly; even the most speculative retail investors are losing interest in crypto-only listings. This means the future role of exchanges will change. Previously, exchanges could reap substantial profits through primary and secondary listings; in the future, they are more likely to revert to providing secondary service, such as transaction fees, custody, and fiat currency access. Like Coinbase, whose core advantage is ETF custody; Kraken, whose advantage is Euro fiat currency deposits and withdrawals and foreign exchange channels. In the next three to five years, the status of each exchange will largely depend on their capabilities in secondary market services. So, to summarize: the crypto market isn't finished, it's maturing. But what does maturity mean? It means less exorbitant profits, less innovation, and the intervention of traditional financial forces. Some old models, such as those relying solely on crypto native listings, may face profound changes, or even marginalization. The industry can survive, and will survive even more stably, but the days of champagne and sports cars are probably gone forever. Cindy: The era of a purely crypto market is over; stablecoins and blockchain-based US stocks are driving deep integration of crypto and traditional finance. Cindy: Is the crypto market finished? I agree at least halfway. First, the era of a purely crypto market is definitely over. This is evident from the transformation actions of major exchanges. Why are Binance and Bitget both deploying blockchain-based US stocks? Matrixport also launched the function of depositing stablecoins to buy US stocks two weeks ago. They possess the most authentic, firsthand user data. When Bitcoin reached around $120,000, large investors were already selling their coins and turning to the US stock market to seek AI-related investment opportunities. If exchanges don't upgrade their product structure, high-net-worth users will quickly leave. It's no longer the stage where maintaining relationships with a few large clients is enough to retain customers. However, on the other hand, I believe the era of integration between traditional industries and Crypto has arrived. A clear example is stablecoins. In the past six months, through blind dates, I've met many people in the payment industry. They often complain that stablecoins are going to "kill" the traditional payment industry. For the past decade or so, the payment industry has made a lot of money through channel fees, exchange rate differences, and information asymmetry. However, since the emergence of stablecoins, leveraging the exchange ecosystem, they have achieved large-scale distribution from 0 to 1, and from 1 to 100. Customers have also gradually realized that using stablecoins for settlement is faster, cheaper, and more transparent. Therefore, they have begun to require traditional payment companies to integrate stablecoins. The day before yesterday, I invited leading self-media figures in the payment industry to our company for an offline sharing session. Around 11 pm the night before, people from exchanges, asset management, wallets, and OTC teams all contacted me at the last minute, asking if they could attend. It's clear that there is indeed a strong need for integration between the cryptocurrency industry and the traditional payment industry. Moreover, everyone is currently under great pressure to meet performance KPIs and is looking for new growth points. As for which projects are thriving? The first category consists of projects that capitalize on local policy benefits. For example, teams adept at utilizing Hong Kong's policy window and quickly achieving compliant implementation. The second category comprises Hong Kong-listed companies that have timely transformed and integrated into the new narrative. These companies, possessing both traditional capital market identities and the ability to embrace the crypto narrative, are thriving. Therefore, my view is that while the era of a purely "crypto circle" is indeed ending, the era of crypto integration with traditional finance has only just begun. Esther: The Rise of Policy Dividends and American Backing – The Real Danger is a “Mental Bear Market” Esther: I think those who are doing well are those who have seized policy dividends. For example, on February 6th, the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC) issued new regulations allowing the tokenization of high-quality domestic assets. This is essentially a policy tailwind. Previously, we were always being “fleeced,” but now we can also bring out our high-quality assets and connect with global offshore liquidity. For projects like ours that have always followed the compliant route and are relatively “well-behaved,” this is a very good window of opportunity. Finally, there is a clear framework to conduct business, instead of groping in the gray areas. Which ones will "go under"? The first category is those with severe path dependence. For example, the quantitative trading teams that Albert just mentioned were wiped out. If they don't upgrade their strategies, they will still run into problems when faced with systemic risks next time. Similarly, there are some project teams and agencies. If they continue to operate using the methods from the last bull market, they will basically not make money. The last bull market was a special phase with extremely high liquidity and strong narratives. Replicating that approach now will definitely not work. I know many offshore exchanges are also trying to transform, otherwise they will struggle greatly. There are also projects that chase hot trends. As Kirara said, it's like a talent show. The window of opportunity for trending topics is only a few months. If you don't release a product when it's at its peak, it's almost impossible to create a pre-product after the hype dies down. For example, in the prediction market, there are 800 trending topics every day; it's simply impossible to keep up. I think there are three types of people who believe the crypto market won't collapse, and may even be thriving. The first type is entry-level businesses with clear business models and cash flow. For example, Radopay is about to go public, and its valuation is said to be close to $2 billion. Many of these companies don't actually want to be labeled as "Web3 companies." Because once labeled as a "crypto company," it might actually affect their valuation. I think this is quite sad, but it's the reality. Projects like NPC Wallet, which serve businesses, have real demand and are therefore more likely to develop steadily. The second category consists of what I call "American native" projects. These naturally occupy the top of the food chain, especially those with strong political and financial backing. For example, LayerZero; I happened to attend their dinner. They've recently announced many collaborations, such as with Citadel (which accounts for about 20% of US stock market trading volume) and DTCC. With the support of Wall Street veterans like a16z and Franklin Templeton, these projects naturally possess pricing power and industry influence. The third category is securities firms. Many traditional securities firms are involved this time, and they have a very positive attitude. For them, it's simply about completing a VATP license upgrade and adding a new profit point. For example, selling cryptocurrencies can generate slippage and commissions, with returns even higher than traditional stock trading. You'll see securities firms already promoting cryptocurrency sales in Central and Tsim Sha Tsui in Hong Kong. They have mature front-end traffic capabilities, operational capabilities, and VIP service systems. They don't actually think the cryptocurrency market is doomed. Finally, I want to summarize. When I did a podcast with Professor Mingdao before, he said something: The most terrifying thing is not a price bear market, but a psychological bear market. Price drops aren't scary; what's scary is that some established players have already made enough money and are numb to industry changes, or they're xenophobic, refusing to accept new players and changes. If the entire industry enters a "mental bear market," that's truly dangerous. Nana: Inferior projects are being cleared out, institutions are dominating the market, retail opportunities are shrinking, but the industry is healthier. Nana: Okay. I'll mainly talk about it from two perspectives: one is the project owner's perspective, and the other is the investor's perspective. First, let's talk about the project owners. Because I've worked on projects myself, I'm quite clear on this. In the early days of the cryptocurrency market, there were indeed many opportunistic projects. Many projects hadn't even developed a product, or were just a PowerPoint presentation; as long as they got endorsements from some well-known labs, they could issue tokens and then reap the profits. However, this narrative no longer works. Overall liquidity is very low now. VCs have less money, and many VCs are no longer investing much in the primary market. If your product isn't solid, can't generate cash flow, or the founding team doesn't have genuine fundraising capabilities, the project will be very difficult to launch. I actually think this is a good thing. It's a process of natural selection, which will improve the overall quality of the industry and allow users to use better products. From an investment perspective, with more and more institutions entering the market, I personally feel it's not as friendly to retail investors as before. It's not that there are no opportunities at all, but there are far fewer opportunities than before. For example, in mainstream cryptocurrencies, a large portion of the tokens are actually held by large institutions. Ordinary retail investors don't hold many tokens; many are currently holding USDT and observing the market. If tokens are highly concentrated in the hands of institutions, their ability to control market fluctuations will be stronger. In this situation, many retail investors will turn their attention to Meme coin. However, I personally believe that after last year's market volatility and various FUD events, enthusiasm for Meme has clearly declined, and people have become more cautious. Overall, the market is now more rational and more institutionalized. Short-term profit opportunities for retail investors have decreased, but in the long run, the industry may be healthier. Haotian: The sentiment is "finished," but the Agentic Economy may become the next consensus. Haotian: Let me state my conclusion first—the sentiment is definitely "finished." There's no need to shy away from this. The past two years have been agonizing for both long-term holders and those with faith in the industry. Project teams are suffering, and VCs are suffering even more. At conferences, you can clearly feel that the overall sentiment has indeed collapsed. Why? Because the old narratives and old rules are becoming ineffective. Many secondary market retail investors are still stuck in the previous cycle, always hoping to win tenfold or a hundredfold. But in this cycle, you'll find that the more you cling to this fantasy, the more you lose. The same applies to project teams. In the previous two cycles, writing a white paper, building a token for two years, and achieving a valuation of three to five hundred million US dollars was not difficult. But now, valuations have been severely compressed. What does this mean? It means that this circle is not as attractive as it used to be. Objectively speaking, AI is now more "respectable" than the crypto world. If you start an AI startup in Web2, it's easier to raise funds, and the growth potential is clearer. If you want to raise money, buying AI concept stocks on the US stock market is likely less risky than buying altcoins. So why has it become like this? First, there are too many assets, and token issuance has become industrialized and streamlined. It's not just Meme. Previously, Meme tokens could last for months, but now that has shrunk to weeks, days, or even minutes. The lifespan is getting shorter and shorter; this is a result of involution. Project teams are increasingly adopting the "fundraising - TGE" model, making it more and more like an assembly line. During TGE, many products weren't mature yet; it's essentially still an attention economy. The entire industry seems to be playing the attention arbitrage game. The era of "buying a few and waiting for a general rise, holding on and getting rich quick" is basically gone. The core reason is—a breakdown in consensus. Why are OGs silent now? Some have indeed made enough money and are free; others may have lost money and left. But more importantly, they don't understand the current game. Every six months, there are new KOLs, new projects, and new narratives. The older generation may not be able to keep up. Exchanges are also changing. Binance, for example, has now become the focus of online discussion. Other exchanges are also making their own moves, like Bitget tokenizing US stocks and OKX developing AI Agents. Instead of building a liquidity loop around native on-chain assets, everyone is going their own way. This makes the "general rise logic" of native on-chain assets increasingly difficult. Hyperliquid, for example, is one of the few projects that has succeeded in this round. However, it's far removed from the market, focusing solely on product development, and unable to leverage KOLs. Such teams are rare. The same applies to prediction markets. Polymarket dominates MindShares, with valuations often reaching $30 billion. How do newcomers price their offerings? You can't claim to be anchored to Polymarket; that would be ridiculed. Many native prediction markets in the crypto world are essentially just about leveraging liquidity. Looking at the technical narrative, ZK, high-performance Layer 1, and BTC Layer 2 are almost never discussed anymore. There's a sense of tragic grandeur, like the mighty Yangtze flowing eastward. The founders are still working diligently, but seem to have been abandoned by the times. Many problems exist, but are there new opportunities? I've been talking about a direction lately—the Agentic Economy. Why do I think this is an opportunity? Because AI consensus is taking shape. Innovation is still happening. Things like new ERC standards, the expansion of stablecoins, prediction markets, AI trading, and Robotics can actually be connected. Robotics is Physical AI, the skeleton and limbs of AI. Stablecoins are the infrastructure for value transfer. Prediction markets are information pricing mechanisms. AI trading is automated execution. Put these together, and you have a grand framework for the Agentic Economy. Add to that narratives like digital avatars, digital immortality, and AI proxy personalities, and this forms a comprehensive macro-level direction that can span both Web2 and Web3. I don't think the next cycle will revert to purely speculative logic, but I believe new and interesting things will emerge around the Agentic Economy. So, to summarize: the sentiment is indeed "finished," the old narrative is receding, but a new AI+Crypto macro-narrative may be forming. The key is whether we can find a new consensus after the collapse of the old one. Teddy: Reflections after the Consensus Conference — Lack of innovation is the root of the crisis; Agentic Economy may become the new consensus. Teddy: Let me start with a fundamental question. Before answering the question "Is the crypto world finished?", let's ask a question first—what is the core of the crypto world? I think the core is decentralization, including decentralized technology and the spirit of decentralization. These two points have always been valuable. So, essentially, decentralization cannot be finished; it is meaningful in the long run. But the reality is that the entire crypto world is facing a very serious problem—people feel it's useless. A large number of "useless assets" have been generated in the market, faith is waning, and funds and builders are leaving in large numbers. Of course, not everyone has left, but the rate of loss is very high. I've been thinking about the reasons. I think the core problem is the lack of genuine innovation in recent years. Looking back at history, Bitcoin was born in 2008, marking the first major innovation—a decentralized peer-to-peer electronic cash system. Over a decade of validation has proven its value. The second major innovation was Ethereum, which introduced the concepts of a "world computer" and smart contracts. This generated immense excitement at the time. The third was the DeFi Summer of 2020, which gave rise to a series of truly useful protocols such as Uniswap and Aave. To this day, these protocols remain the cornerstone of the entire on-chain ecosystem. It is precisely because of innovations like Bitcoin, Ethereum, and DeFi that we have today's RWA, the large-scale application of stablecoins, and the convergence of Web2 and Web3. However, in this cycle, we haven't seen a new product with the same impact. As a result, a large number of ineffective assets have appeared on the market, and many project teams no longer aim to create great products or build sustainable cash flow, but rather to "list on top exchanges." When listing becomes the sole objective, the industry will run into problems. This is because many projects stop building as soon as they are listed, lacking any real value. A typical alpha in this cycle has even become—shorting from the day the token is listed. Many tokens reach their highest point on the first day of listing, so who would be willing to hold them long-term? When an asset itself lacks long-term value, the market naturally loses confidence. Prices fall, and BTC, ETH, and DeFi tokens all come under pressure. Builders leave, VCs leave, incubators decrease, and the supply side decreases. The supply of truly high-quality assets is decreasing, which is a serious crisis. So where is the future? I've recently been focusing on prediction markets and AI. Prediction markets have value, but they haven't yet brought the same kind of shock as Bitcoin, Ethereum, and DeFi. What will truly make a difference is AI. For example, with current AI programming capabilities (Vibe Coding), you can generate a usable product from an idea in just over ten minutes. Previously, even a strong development team would need one to two weeks. This productivity improvement is a huge leap. Another example is the new generation of large language models, which not only "help you analyze problems," but can directly "help you solve problems." You say a sentence, and it executes it for you; this is already close to a true AI assistant or digital employee. Therefore, I strongly agree with the Agentic Economy direction. If Crypto is to achieve new consensus, it may have to combine with the Agentic Economy. Another direction is decentralized AI. Current AI is extremely centralized, with models controlled by a few companies and operating as a black box. In the future, there will likely be increasing emphasis on decentralized AI—a transparent, verifiable, and collectively owned model trained and inferred through decentralized networks. This direction is very early, but in the long run, it is highly consistent with the spirit of decentralization. If a democratic, open, transparent, and human-controlled AI system can be achieved, it will be a highly attractive long-term direction. In summary: The essence of the cryptocurrency market is not dead, but the lack of innovation has led to a crisis of faith. If we want to form a new "consensus" at the next Consensus Conference, then truly impactful new products must emerge. Currently, the most likely breakthrough lies in the Agentic Economy and decentralized AI. Leon: The Consensus Conference is clearly international, but the sectors are relatively quiet; AI trading and prediction markets may become the new direction for consensus. Leon: Because I'm based in Hong Kong, and we participated as a project team, I can share my observations and views on the industry from the perspective of an exhibitor. I think this Consensus Conference was quite international. CoinDesk's organizational capabilities are indeed excellent. While the overall foot traffic wasn't as large as at 2049, there were actually more overseas faces. Whether it was project teams, exhibitors, or attendees, Chinese and overseas people were roughly evenly split. We attended some Demo Days and clearly felt the international atmosphere. We also co-hosted a side event with Google, and most of the attendees were also from overseas. So, frankly, it's a bit of a shame; if this were a bull market, such a platform bridging Asia and the world would be incredibly powerful. We also met many overseas KOLs, channel partners, institutions, and even Wall Street investment bankers looking for projects. However, the problem is that this time it was indeed rather lukewarm. Not many project teams came; many people who came to our booth said there were very few interesting projects. Besides exchanges and service providers, there weren't many truly crypto-native projects seriously developing products; it was mostly service-oriented companies. Many people felt the ticket price wasn't worth it. Moreover, community KOLs rarely attended the event. Surprisingly, aside from us, there were almost no prediction market-related projects at the entire venue. Given how hot the prediction market sector is, there were virtually no related teams at this Consensus Conference. AI projects were also scarce, with only a few robotics-related projects. Another amusing point was that the hackathon's sponsor was a Base-based Meme Launchpad, but it was very quiet. Everyone got bored by the end of the hackathon, and even the first and second place winners left without staying to receive their prizes. This reflects that Meme is indeed no longer popular. Overall, the atmosphere of this bear market is quite strong, somewhat reminiscent of the wave in 2019. During the 2022 bear market, even with price drops, there were still many innovations, such as SocialFi and GameFi, and later Inscription and BRC-20, which still enjoyed popularity. However, in this current bull market, there hasn't been much real innovation, and the atmosphere has worsened since the bear market began. But I still think there are opportunities. I personally favor three areas. First, contracts, whether CEX or DEX perpetual contracts. The saying goes, "bull market spot trading, bear market contracts." Even in a bear market, there's still volatility, allowing for long and short positions and leverage. Trading contracts around major cryptocurrencies offers more opportunities than chasing short-lived coins like Meme or altcoins with 10-minute lifecycles. Moreover, a current trend is that stocks, commodities, and forex are all moving towards on-chain derivatives. Both exchanges and DEXs like Hyperliquid are starting to list these assets. Many traditional retail investors find it difficult to short gold or trade with leverage in the real world, but they can do so on-chain. This is the advantage of combining crypto with traditional assets. Secondly, there's AI. AI has been incredibly hot this year. Whether it's exploring protocol layers like ERC-8004 and EIP-402 within the industry, or deploying open-source LLMs and agents, everyone is involved. Outside the industry, AI is already disruptive in film, creation, and productivity. The key to the future is how AI can be combined with crypto. Thirdly, there's the prediction market. Prediction markets are still in their early stages, and people haven't yet found a way to participate on a large scale. But it's not just a game within the crypto world; institutions outside the crypto sphere are also watching. And since it's an on-chain application, those of us with on-chain experience have a natural advantage. Overall, I think that despite the strong bear market atmosphere, there's still a lot of interesting stuff that can be created around contracts, AI, and prediction markets. <p style="text-align:center"><strong mpa-from-tpl="t"><span leaf=""><img src="https://img.jinse.com.cn/133310_image3.png"/></span></strong></p><p style="text-align: left;"><strong><strong><span leaf="">To get Wu Shuo news and in-depth content, you can search for the Wu Shuo APP on the Apple/Wu Shuo official website; search for Wu Shuo audio and video on platforms such as Xiaoyuzhou; search for Wu Shuo communication on the Knowledge Planet platform; for media inquiries and business cooperation, please add WeChat customer service ipo19841984;</span></strong></strong><strong><strong><span leaf=""> How to set a starred account on the WuShuo WeChat Official Account; Experience the new WuShuo AI Assistant feature on the Official Account. data.title: Golden Morning Briefing | WLFI Becomes a Warning Signal for the Market; US Institutions Bullish on BTC
data.description:
Headline

▌Report: WLFI May Be an “Early Warning Sign” of a Crypto Market Collapse

According to a research report by data provider Amberdata, WLFI showed a significant early warning effect in the massive market liquidation on October 10, 2025. More than five hours before approximately $6.93 billion in leveraged positions were liquidated across the entire network, WLFI had already begun to fall sharply, while the price of Bitcoin remained around $121,000, showing no obvious pressure. The research suggests that because many trading platforms support WLFI as collateral, the token's sharp fluctuations led to a reduction in the value of the collateral, forcing traders to liquidate BTC and ETH to cover their positions, thus triggering a chain reaction across the entire market. Mike Marshall of Amberdata stated that this five-hour lead time is difficult to consider a coincidence, as WLFI's high leverage makes it more sensitive to market pressures.

Analysis: US Institutions Remain Bullish on BTC, While Overseas Investors Are Withdrawing. According to analysis by Greg Cipolaro, Head of Research at NYDIG, there is a divergence in sentiment between US institutional investors and offshore traders in the Bitcoin market. Currently, the annualized basis for CME Bitcoin futures is higher than that of the offshore exchange Deribit, indicating that US hedge funds and other institutions still prefer to pay a premium to maintain long positions, while interest in leveraged long-term exposure in the offshore market has clearly declined. Regarding previous market rumors that the "quantum computing threat" caused Bitcoin to fall to $60,000, NYDIG believes that the data does not support this logic. Recent Bitcoin price movements have shown a positive correlation with quantum computing-related stocks such as IONQ and D-Wave, rather than an inverse divergence. If quantum computing did indeed pose a targeted threat, related stocks should rise when Bitcoin falls. The current synchronized decline reflects a decrease in the market's overall risk appetite for long-term growth assets. Furthermore, Google Trends data shows that the increase in related searches usually coincides with rising rather than falling cryptocurrency prices, indicating that the topic is more driven by market enthusiasm than panic selling. As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $68,885.51, with a 24-hour change of approximately -1.26%; ETH price is $1,969.99, with a 24-hour change of approximately -5.35%; BNB price is $616.29, with a 24-hour change of approximately... 0);">-2.49%；

Paul Atkins emphasized the cooperation between the SEC and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) on "landmark legislation."

Yat Siu: Institutions are Buying Cryptocurrencies, But Cryptocurrency Users are Buying Institutional Products

Animoca Brands co-founder and executive chairman Yat Siu stated that institutions are buying cryptocurrencies, but cryptocurrency users are buying institutional products.

A break above $71,500 could open a path to $79,000. Data: Polygon Payment Transfers Show Monthly Growth, January Transaction Count Approaching 2 Million Analyst @obchakevich_ reports on the X platform that Polygon payment transfers show monthly growth, with January transaction count approaching 2 million. It should be noted that just two months ago, this figure was less than 1 million. The main reasons for the growth include: the rapid adoption of payment AI agents on Polygon, which has driven the growth of the "micro" transaction category; at the same time, the increase in the number of card products in various innovative payment solutions has also boosted the "small" and "medium" transaction categories. I expect the relevant data to continue to grow significantly in February, with the number of transactions potentially reaching approximately 2.3 million. data.title: How long does PayPal have left before AI takes over the "shopping path"?
data.description:
Author: LUKE SPILL, FintechBlueprint; Translation: Peggy, BlockBeats

As AI agents begin to replace humans in product discovery, decision-making, and order placement, the traditional e-commerce funnel is being rapidly compressed, and payment is no longer the end point of a transaction, but rather part of the embedded infrastructure. This article uses PayPal's acquisition of Cymbio as a starting point to analyze the new competitive landscape under the rise of Agentic Commerce: Google and Shopify are attempting to control the routing layer with UCP, OpenAI and Stripe are seizing the agent execution layer through ACP, and PayPal is striving to shift from a "payment button" to a key node in the "business workflow."

For fintech companies like PayPal and Stripe, the ability to embed the underlying protocols of AI commerce will determine whether they can continue to be at the table; and for the banking and crypto industries, the window of opportunity is equally short.

The following is the original text: Last week, PayPal acquired Cymbio, a platform that helps merchants complete sales across various AI interfaces, including Microsoft Copilot and Perplexity. Market sources estimate the deal is worth between $150 million and $200 million. This is widely seen as a key strategic move by PayPal to remain competitive in the Agentic Commerce sector. Therefore, as AI agents continue to compress and restructure the traditional e-commerce funnel, PayPal is shifting from a typical Web2 payment tool to more upstream and core business processes such as product discovery, catalog distribution, and order orchestration. This shift almost perfectly confirms our analysis from January of this year regarding exponential growth, the power law effect, and increasing returns to scale in Agentic Commerce. Meanwhile, the industry's infrastructure is rapidly taking shape: Google and Shopify are pushing the Universal Commerce Protocol (UCP); OpenAI and Stripe are collaborating on the Agentic Commerce Protocol (ACP); and Microsoft is embedding its checkout capabilities directly into Copilot. The shopping infrastructure centered around "machines," rather than "human users," is being rewritten at an unprecedented pace. Agentic Commerce is delivering on exponential growth expectations in a real-world way. The predictions from various parties are both astonishing and converging: McKinsey predicts that by the end of this decade, Agentic Commerce is expected to generate $1 trillion in revenue in the US retail market, accounting for about one-third of all online retail sales. Morgan Stanley predicts that by 2030, Agentic Commerce will drive U.S. e-commerce spending to $190 billion to $385 billion, representing a market penetration rate of 10%–20%. Bain predicts that by 2030, the Agentic Commerce market will reach $300 billion to $500 billion, accounting for approximately 15%–25% of total online retail sales. Existing adoption data indicates that we are at the inflection point of an exponential growth curve: by November 2025, 23% of US consumers had already used AI to complete a purchase. Cymbio may become PayPal's "middle layer" in AI commerce. For PayPal, Cymbio's potential role is as a middle infrastructure layer in its AI business system. Its core value proposition includes:

Synchronizing product catalogs across different markets and channels

Real-time inventory availability management

Roading orders to merchants' existing OMS (Order Management System) and fulfillment systems

Allowing merchants to continue acting as the legal entity (Merchant of Record) in transactions

Among them, the Store Sync product allows merchants' product catalogs to be directly discovered by AI agents such as Microsoft Copilot and Perplexity, and is expected to be integrated with ChatGPT and Google Gemini in the future.
The prerequisite for AI agents to complete transactions is that product data, prices, inventory, and fulfillment information must be machine-readable and highly reliable. From "Checkout" to "Agentic Commerce Workflow" PayPal processes over $1.7 trillion in payments annually, with over 142 million monthly active accounts. In the traditional model, PayPal's core leverage point is the moment the payment occurs. However, in the Agentic Commerce system, AI systems can handle product discovery, solution comparison, and even direct order placement for users, while PayPal handles identity verification and payment authorization. After integrating Cymbio, PayPal now covers the entire chain: Discovery: Products are recommended and presented by an AI agent. Decisioning: Options are continuously narrowed down through conversational interaction. Checkout: PayPal handles identity verification and payment. Fulfilment: Orders are directly injected into the merchant's system for execution. Protocol Battle: Service vs. Standards. While PayPal is advancing Agentic Commerce as a "product and service," Google and Shopify are building a cross-functional, standardized Agentic Commerce protocol system. The key point is: Google is embedding UCP (Universal Commerce Protocol) directly into Search and Gemini. Shopify, on the other hand, ensures that its millions of merchants only need to integrate once to reach multiple AI agents. This means that the underlying infrastructure of AI commerce is evolving from "single-point capabilities" to a "protocol-based network." The goal of UCP is to control the "routing layer" of AI commerce, rather than directly owning or operating the commerce itself. This is more of a defensive strategy: by making this layer a "free" public protocol and introducing strong network effects, it prevents any single competitor from monopolizing core control of the AI ​​business ecosystem. Therefore, PayPal is not directly competing with UCP, but rather actively embedding itself into this system. Google has explicitly stated that UCP-based checkout capabilities will support multiple payment service providers, including PayPal and Google Pay. In other words, UCP attempts to be a "neutral highway," while PayPal hopes to become an indispensable tollbooth and payment node on this highway. OpenAI and Stripe are the main competitors in this field. Back in September, Stripe and OpenAI announced the launch of Instant Checkout in ChatGPT, powered by the Agentic Commerce Protocol (ACP). ACP allows AI agents to proactively initiate purchase requests via a structured API, with Stripe issuing shared payment tokens to confirm payments under agent authorization. This enables AI, once authorized, to complete the entire transaction process on behalf of the user, from order placement to payment. Stripe subsequently launched the Agentic Commerce Suite in December 2025, enabling merchants to: Publish product catalogs for direct access by AI agents; and choose which agents to use. AI Agents Conduct Sales

Payments, Risk Control, and Dispute Resolution are Handled Through Stripe

Order Events are Reported Back to Existing Business Systems

Stripe processed over $1 trillion in payments in 2024, serving millions of businesses worldwide. Its competitive strategy is very clear: to become the "default wallet" and "action execution layer" for AI agents—a path highly similar to its early days as the default payment API for internet companies.

In this context, PayPal and Stripe are clearly clashing head-on:
The two are vying not only for payments themselves, but also for the key control points when AI agents actually "execute transactions."

Comparing the three systems together (This usually involves a horizontal comparison of UCP/ACP/PayPal + Cymbio: Who controls the routing layer, who controls the protocol, and who controls payment and fulfillment—and the sources of their respective network effects.) If you'd like, I can directly organize the next paragraph into a comparison table or a highly summarized "strategic assessment," clearly explaining the division of labor and game among the three parties.

Key Takeaways

Three impacts are particularly prominent:

Business transactions will become conversational and can be delegated.
Purchasing will no longer be a process where users click through steps, but rather AI will understand needs in conversation and complete the process on their behalf with authorization.

Merchants can "integrate once, distribute everywhere."
Merchants do not need to adapt to each platform individually; they only need to complete one integration, and their products can reach users through multiple AI agents and channels.

Payments will become embedded infrastructure, not just the endpoint of transactions. Payments will no longer be the "last step button," but a fundamental capability deeply embedded in the discovery, decision-making, and fulfillment processes. Payment networks' proactive approach: Incidentally, Mastercard announced in January 2026 that it was researching "AI business rules," essentially attempting to take the lead in defining the governance framework for this transformation. Payment networks clearly recognize that the power to set rules and standards will determine their future position before AI agents complete transactions on a large scale. As we pointed out in our analysis this January: banks, fintech companies, and the crypto industry must ensure they are "at the table," not incorporated afterward. If financial institutions fail to embed themselves in these platforms in advance, their financial functions may ultimately be internalized by Big Tech.

The Situation and Choices of Different Camps

For Banks

Traditional banks lack the technological infrastructure to directly compete with Google, OpenAI, or Microsoft at the Agentic Commerce level. However, they still possess three key resources: payment clearing channels, customer credit relationships, and compliance and regulatory experience.

These assets determine that banks will not disappear, but must reposition themselves.

For Fintech Companies

Companies such as PayPal, Stripe, and Adyen realized early on that simply providing payments is no longer sufficient to solidify their long-term position.

Therefore, they are actively moving upstream, entering: commerce orchestration, merchant services, and the infrastructure layer of the AI ​​era. For the crypto industry (For Crypto): The Agentic Commerce protocol system announced so far is almost entirely based on traditional financial pathways: credit cards, Google Pay, PayPal, Stripe, etc., occupy core positions. In UCP, ACP, and Store Sync, cryptocurrencies and stablecoins are largely absent, except for some sporadic experiments involving Stripe or Coinbase. Whether this is a significant strategic oversight or a deliberate exclusion remains to be seen. For crypto companies, the window of opportunity is very clear: if they can build a payment track natively adapted to AI agents (instant settlement, programmable money, global availability) and successfully embed it into an AI platform before the protocol is fully solidified, they have the potential to leapfrog traditional finance; otherwise, they may be permanently excluded from the system. In conclusion, PayPal is fundamentally trying to catch up with Stripe and adapt to rapidly changing consumer behavior. As people increasingly make daily decisions within AI platforms, these platforms will gradually evolve into "default virtual showcases" for brands. Whoever can embed themselves into the infrastructure behind these showcases will remain at the table. PayPal's stock price has been sluggish for some time, down about 37% from its 52-week high. Investors are increasingly questioning whether the company retains its structural relevance in the long term, and the rise of the Crypto + AI narrative has only exacerbated these concerns. Against this backdrop, the diversification around Agentic Commerce is not an aggressive move, but rather a "necessary cost" to maintain relevance. For PayPal, this is not icing on the cake, but an unavoidable entry fee: only by completing this shift can it remain at the core of the next generation of commerce infrastructure.

data.title: Polymarket vs. Kalshi: A Timeline of the Prediction Market Meme Battle
data.description:
Author: Hunter Ryerson, Pirate Wires; Translator: Peggy, BlockBeats

Editor's Note: From free grocery stores to a meme war of mutual mockery, the competition between Polymarket and Kalshi has long since transcended product and market share battles, becoming a meticulously crafted war for attention. This article traces the timeline of the rivalry between the two prediction market platforms over the years: regulatory battles, user bans and returns, "competitive meme-playing" on social media, and ultimately, a marketing spectacle that spills over into the real world. Behind the seemingly absurd and dramatic maneuvers lies a continuous rise in trading volume, valuation, and capital expectations.

The following is the original text:

February 12th, morning, Manhattan. You wake up in a cramped, shoebox-sized apartment that costs $2,000 a month, turn on the heat, and drag yourself to the pantry to see if you can scrape together some breakfast.

Then you remember: at 3 a.m. last night, you heated up and ate the last packet of instant ramen. Just as you're hesitating about whether to continue contributing 20% ​​of your income to DoorDash, a friend texts you, telling you about a new grocery store called "The Polymarket" that opened on Madison Street—and everything inside is free. So, naturally, you put on your pants, stroll over to Lower Manhattan, successfully squeeze into the store, and instantly enter "Black Friday shopping spree" mode, frantically buying everything in sight, grabbing everything your debt-ridden hands can reach. As you walk home with bulging shopping bags, stuffed full of Sour Patch Kids gummies and vegetables you haven't seen in weeks, tucked under your arm, you pass a billboard advertising a $50 free grocery credit at an East Village market sponsored by a company called "Kalshi." Am I dreaming? How could I be so lucky? Congratulations, you've been caught up in the latest wave of prediction market advertising. That's right. Just this week, the wildly popular prediction market platform Polymarket announced its latest marketing gimmick: a "completely free grocery store" in New York City, open from February 12th to February 16th. Meanwhile, its competitor, another prediction market, Kalshi, wasn't to be outdone, launching its own "grocery-themed" marketing campaign: giving away $50 in general grocery subsidies to everyone for one day at Westside Market on Third Avenue. This back-and-forth imitation prompted one user, X, to complain: "Kalshi and Polymarket can't even stand 24 hours a day; they copy everything." At first glance, it seems absurd that Polymarket and Kalshi would compete in marketing with "free bananas." But this is actually just the latest episode in the long-standing feud between the two platforms. It's important to understand that their weekly trading volume reaches billions of dollars. Their business model, simply put, is this: people can bet on the outcome of seemingly harmless events for extremely high returns—for example, whether the US military will arrest Venezuelan leader Nicolás Maduro while he's wearing casual pajamas. (That night, some unknown, but hopefully not Pete Hegseth, cyber spies made a fortune.) In short, in their short five-year history, these two prediction market platforms have always been competitors, but the real fierce competition has only fully erupted in the last two years. Polymarket, founded in 2020 by NYU dropout Shayne Coplan, is a cryptocurrency-based platform. Bettors need to deposit USDC, a stablecoin equivalent to the US dollar, on the Polygon blockchain to purchase "yes" or "no" prediction shares. In contrast, Kalshi initially operated almost entirely in US dollars, with transactions and fund deposits and withdrawals completed through traditional bank accounts. Since its launch in 2021, it has primarily focused on sports betting, which accounts for 90% of the platform's total trading volume. Polymarket, on the other hand, leans more towards geopolitical and cultural events, such as wars, conflicts, and elections, and even pays American influencers to promote its political content. From 2022 to 2025, due to strong regulatory pressure from the US Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) (and a $1.4 million fine), Polymarket temporarily banned US users from its platform, effectively handing over the US market to Kalshi for several years. However, just a few months ago, Polymarket returned to the US market, reigniting the fierce conflict between the two companies, with various frictions unfolding on the X platform and even in the wider cyberspace. For netizens who are "always online," the most entertaining part of this war is undoubtedly the so-called "competitive shitposting." In the sports world, this strategy manifests as parodies of official sports announcement cards—the kind you often see on ESPN or FOX Sports accounts used to announce trades, drafts, or injuries. Both platforms use attention-grabbing, joking headlines to "report" sports news. For example, Polymarket's "DICK IS GROWING" alludes to Toronto Raptors player Gradey Dick's weight gain; and Kalshi's "LOVES RECEIVING BALLS" refers to San Francisco 49ers running back Christian McCaffrey, saying he "likes to catch the ball"—you know, literally meaning "catching and passing the ball."

But as both sides fought for "meme-style attention," their tactics began to become more unscrupulous.

In November 2024, evidence emerged that Kalshi attempted to pay influencers—such as former NFL wide receiver and current X platform activist Antonio Brown—to publish and spread negative comments about Polymarket (see: "Kalshi Pays Influencers to Attack Polymarket CEO After FBI Raid"). In one case, a journalist was reportedly paid $3,500 to write a smear article against Polymarket.

(As an aside: If Solana were willing to pay me this much to write smear articles, I could impeach Jackie Fielder by Monday.) It's alleged that the influencers Kalshi "managed" have a combined following of millions. For the past few years, they've been trying to gradually erode Polymarket's credibility. Following this incident, the Trump administration relaxed regulations on prediction markets, allowing Polymarket to make a strong comeback in the US market. After months of preparation, the US user ban was finally lifted in December, and now Polymarket is trying to regain a majority share of the on-chain prediction market (currently, after integration with the Solana blockchain, Kalshi still holds a dominant position). One way they've found to "amplify their voice" is by publishing news on X. Over the past few months, Polymarket and Kalshi's brand accounts have been locked in a timeline battle, vying for the top spot with concise, powerful headlines and celebrity quotes—sometimes disregarding accuracy or any other principles. Recently, Polymarket mistakenly attributed a statement to Jeff Bezos and significantly exaggerated deportation data; while Kalshi spread false information about negotiations to acquire Greenland. Ultimately, this feud has spilled over from the online world into the real world, and hopefully, it will continue to have some positive "spillover effects" for ordinary Americans like you who are strapped for cash. But the real point is this: whether it's a marketing gimmick like "free groceries" or the mutual mockery and backstabbing on the X platform, both companies are extremely adept at generating buzz and keeping people talking about them. No matter how questionable their tactics to outdo each other—whether they seem shady or strangely generous—we're constantly discussing it. Perhaps this is exactly what they want. Kalshi and Polymarket are valued at $11 billion and $9 billion respectively, and they're still climbing at an astonishing rate. So, as long as the drama in this crazy war attracts a few hundred more bettors or a few more investors, it's all worth it. It's a win-win for these two "duelers," if you're willing to believe it.
